fact: The SS Edmund Fitzgerald was the largest ship on the Great Lakes at the time of her sinking
source: National Museum of the Great Lakes / widely documented; she was the largest when launched in 1958 and remained among the largest at the time of her loss

fact: The Fitzgerald was carrying twenty-six thousand tons of iron ore (taconite pellets)
source: U.S. National Transportation Safety Board, Marine Accident Report: SS Edmund Fitzgerald Sinking in Lake Superior, November 10, 1975 (NTSB-MAR-78-3)

fact: Captain McSorley radioed that a fence rail was down and two vents were lost or damaged, exposing the hull to flooding
source: U.S. Coast Guard Marine Casualty Report: SS Edmund Fitzgerald; Sinking in Lake Superior, 10 November 1975 (Report No. USCG 16732/64216)

fact: Waves on Lake Superior reached approximately 35 feet during the storm on November 10, 1975
source: NTSB Marine Accident Report MAR-78-3; the National Weather Service recorded sustained winds of 60+ knots and wave heights estimated at 25–35 feet

fact: All twenty-nine crew members were lost and the ship sank rapidly, with no distress signal or survivors
source: U.S. Coast Guard Marine Casualty Report and NTSB Report MAR-78-3; widely documented

fact: No voyage data recorder was carried aboard the Fitzgerald, and the exact cause of the sinking was never definitively confirmed
source: NTSB Marine Accident Report MAR-78-3; the NTSB and USCG reached differing conclusions, and VDRs were not required on Great Lakes vessels at the time
